address: 158 Thanon Wang Doem, Wat Arun, Bangkok Yai
best_time_to_visit: Late afternoon (16:00-18:00) when the sun illuminates the western facade and you can stay for the spectacular sunset over the Chao Phraya River

description.en:
## Overview

Wat Arun Ratchawararam — the Temple of Dawn — is one of the most recognizable landmarks in Thailand. Its towering central prang (Khmer-style spire) rises 82 meters above the west bank of the Chao Phraya River, covered in an intricate mosaic of colorful porcelain and seashells that glitter in the sunlight. Despite its name, the temple is actually most stunning at sunset, when the fading light paints the spires in gold and amber tones against the darkening sky.

## History

The temple dates back to the Ayutthaya period and was originally known as Wat Makok. After the fall of Ayutthaya in 1767, King Taksin established his new capital at Thonburi and claimed the temple as his royal chapel, renaming it Wat Chaeng — later Wat Arun — after Aruna, the Hindu god of dawn. The iconic central prang was built during the reign of King Rama III (1824-1851) and restored extensively between 2013 and 2017, revealing the original vibrant colors of the porcelain mosaics that had faded over the centuries.

## What to See

The main prang dominates the complex, and visitors can climb steep steps about halfway up for panoramic views of the Grand Palace, Wat Pho, and the Bangkok skyline across the river. The four smaller satellite prangs at each corner feature niches containing statues of Nayu (the wind god) on horseback. Around the base, look for the Chinese ceramic figures of soldiers, animals, and mythological creatures embedded in the stucco. The ordination hall (ubosot) houses a golden Buddha image said to have been designed by King Rama II himself.

## Practical Info

Admission is 200 THB for foreigners. The temple is open daily from 08:00 to 18:00. Dress code requires covered shoulders and knees; wraps are available to rent at the gate for 20-50 THB. The easiest way to reach Wat Arun is by cross-river ferry from Tha Tien Pier near Wat Pho (4 THB, 2-minute ride). Wear shoes you can easily remove, as footwear must come off inside temple buildings. The steep prang steps can be slippery — hold the railings.
description.nl: Tempel van de Dageraad beroemd om zijn torenhoge spitsen en ingewikkelde keramische details

tips: Cross from Tha Tien Pier near Wat Pho on the 4 THB ferry — combine both temples in one half-day trip
Visit between 16:00 and 18:00 for the best photography light as the sun illuminates the porcelain mosaics
The steep prang steps are narrow and slippery — wear shoes with grip and hold the iron railings
